items.q: What is appropriation in postmodern art?
items.correct: Deliberately reusing existing images or styles, often to comment on originality.
items.why: Students moralise about copying. Postmodern appropriation (Sherrie Levine, Richard Prince) is a CRITICAL STRATEGY - it questions authorship and originality, central modernist values.

items.correct: Strong contrast between light and dark to model three-dimensional form.
items.why: Students think it's about subject matter (night scenes). Chiaroscuro is a TECHNIQUE using value contrast to suggest volume - exemplified by Caravaggio but used since the Renaissance to model form.

items.correct: An object's design should be determined by its purpose.
items.misconception: Decoration is the most important aspect of design.
items.random_wrong: Designers must follow strict government forms.
items.why: Students invert the meaning. Sullivan's modernist principle prioritises FUNCTION over ornament - the chair's shape should serve sitting, not the other way around. Postmodernism later challenged this.

items.correct: A mould taken from a positive model
items.why: It's A mould taken from a positive model. “A finished bronze figure” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which artist made the Angel of the North?
items.why: Correct answer: Antony Gormley. “Henry Moore” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Approximate height in metres of the Angel of the North?
items.why: The real figure is 20. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
